raw_case:
Cerebral Artery Vasoconstriction After Galcanezumab Loading Dose for Migraine Prevention: A Case Report
Anti-calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) monoclonal antibodies that target CGRP ligands or receptors, may cause a very rare side effect of reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS). This study is a case report of a patient who developed cerebral artery vasoconstriction documented on serial brain magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) scans without the typical manifestations of RCVS following galcanezumab loading dose. Case report: A 40-year-old female patient with high-frequency episodic migraine with visual aura on topiramate 100 mg/day developed transient numbness of the right upper and lower extremities and right face without headache and a normal neurological examination 10 min after a loading dose of galcanezumab, which resolved over the next 2 days. Magnetic resonance angiography brain imaging showed segmental arterial constriction of both middle cerebral arteries in the M1–2 segments and both posterior cerebral arteries in the P1–2 segments, which partial resolved in a subsequent study by the end of 6 months. There were no other supporting examination data, such as transcranial Doppler, which might provide additional information on the progression and improvement of the vasoconstriction. Her differential diagnosis included prolonged migraine sensory aura without headache, RCVS, or cerebral vasoconstriction secondary to the effect of an anti-CGRP monoclonal antibody. Further research needs to be conducted.

Plain Language Summary

We report a case with numbness on the right upper and lower extremities and right face without headache 10 min after the loading dose of galcanezumab. These stroke-like symptoms resolved within 2 days. Cerebral blood vessels showed narrowing and then dilatation with residual narrowing of two or more vessels. The differential diagnosis is prolonged migraine aura without headache, reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome, or cerebral vasoconstriction due to the effects of galcanezumab.

Key Summary Points




Introduction

Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) is characterized by multifocal arterial constriction and dilation in the cerebral vasculature. RCVS can be associated with non-aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage and can be triggered by exposure to certain drugs [1]. New migraine prevention drugs, such as anti-calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) monoclonal antibodies that block CGRP ligands or receptors, may have a rare side effect of RCVS, which to date has only been reported in three cases in the literature [2, 3]. This case report describes a patient with cerebral vasoconstriction after receiving a loading dose of galcanezumab.
This case report was reviewed and approved by the Chulalongkorn Institutional Review Board (IRB no. 437/67). The patient provided written informed consent to disclose this information, and consent was obtained for publication of potentially identifiable information. The data were retrieved from the patient’s electronic medical record and logbook headache diary.

Case Report

A 40-year-old woman with a history of migraine with aura dating back 6 years has been treated for the last 4 years at the Chulalongkorn Comprehensive Headache Center, King Chulalongkorn Memorial Hospital, a tertiary care hospital in Bangkok, Thailand. Her visual aura involves blurring of the whole visual field for 5 min during the headache phase. She also reported rare difficulties in understanding people who were talking to her for about 1 min during the headache phase. She had comorbid allergic rhinitis and took cetirizine 10 mg/day but did not take any stimulant medications. Her migraine attacks were initially present at most two times per month, with symptoms lasting from 1 to 2 days. Her headache characteristics were non-throbbing pain with a unilateral location that side shifts in the periorbital, forehead, temporal, occipital, and neck regions, accompanied by nausea, photophobia, phonophobia, dizziness, and osmophobia and exacerbated by strong smells and hot weather.Three years ago, she took propranolol 80 mg/day, topiramate 50 mg/day, and nortriptyline 10 mg/day for migraine prevention but stopped taking nortriptyline because of constipation. After the cessation of nortriptyline treatment, the frequency of migraine attacks increased to 5–7 days per month. The dose of topiramate was increased to 75 mg/day and then 100 mg/day. She responded to topiramate 100 mg/day and propranolol 80 mg/day with 3 headache days per month. For acute treatment, she took acetaminophen 500 mg, etoricoxib 60 mg, or naproxen sodium 250 mg plus eletriptan 40 mg. She never had any side effects from these medications.Two years ago, she stopped all migraine prevention as her headaches were well controlled. One month after discontinuation of the migraine prevention, she developed near daily headaches. Topiramate 100 mg/day for migraine prevention was restarted, and her headache frequency improved.One year ago, she developed chronic migraine with headache on > 15 days a month associated with medication overuse of eletriptan 40 mg, which was taken an average of 10–15 days per month, and did not respond to topiramate 100 mg/days. Amitriptyline 10 mg/day for migraine prevention was combined, but her headache frequency did not respond. As a result, the treatment plan was to combine anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies with her oral migraine prevention. Two months before her loading dose of anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies, her monthly migraine days were nine headache days per month, and 1 month before her loading dose her headache frequency was seven headache days a month. In her logbook headache diary, in the last 28 days before the loading dose, she had headache days on 28, 27, 22, 11, 10, 9, and 1 day before the injection. She took acetaminophen 500 mg plus eletriptan 40 mg once a day only on days 28, 27, and 22 before the injection, and the last day of taking acetaminophen 500 mg plus eletriptan 40 mg was 22 days before the injection. On the four recent headache days before the injection, she did not take any analgesics or triptans as her headache severity was mild. Her Thai version of the Migraine Disability Scale (MIDAS) score and the Migraine Specific Quality of Life Questionnaire version 2.1 (MSQ 2.1) score were 23 days and 67.2, respectively. She was a candidate for anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies, according to the Thai guidelines [4], with no response to oral migraine prevention in at least two out of four classes of preventive medications and monthly migraine days of 4–7 days with severe disability (MIDAS score at least 21 days). On the evening before the planned loading dose of galcanezumab, she had a headache with very mild pain severity and did not take any medications.On the day of the injection, her blood pressure, pulse rate, and body mass index were 119/66 mmHg, 90 bpm, and 20.3 kg/m2, respectively. She received a loading dose of galcanezumab, 120 mg × 2, which was injected subcutaneously in both deltoid regions. Ten minutes later, she developed numbness in her right buttock, spreading to the right knee, lateral aspect of the right thigh, right shoulder, lateral aspect of the right arm, right side of the tongue, and right side of the cheek. Twenty minutes later, the numbness spread to her right foot. Neurological examinations revealed normal findings including pinprick sensation, motor functions, and deep tendon reflexes. Thirty minutes later, her numbness slightly improved but did not resolve. She did not experience any headaches during the numbness. She was discharged and subsequently observed at home. On the day of the injection, she neither had a headache nor took any analgesics. The next day, she developed transient bilateral non-pulsatile tinnitus in the morning, and it resolved in the evening. The tinnitus occurred every day. The second day after the injection, she developed a headache and took one tablet of eletriptan 40 mg. Her numbness resolved over 2 days, and bilateral non-pulsatile tinnitus resolved over 2 weeks. Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) brain imaging was performed 24 days after the loading dose and revealed segmental arterial constriction of both middle cerebral arteries (MCA) in the M1–2 segments and both posterior cerebral arteries (PCA) in the P1–2 segments (Fig. 1A,B). She had not had any triptans just prior to this. Four weeks after the injection, she visited the clinic with a scheduled follow-up and reported migraine days for 2 days, the 2nd and 15th days after the injection. She took one tablet of eletriptan 40 mg for each headache.The day the MRA brain imaging was done was 9 days after the last dose of eletriptan treatment. Neurological findings were normal. She was diagnosed with possible RCVS related to galcanezumab and was advised to discontinue galcanezumab injections and not to take triptans. One month after the injection, sequential MRA brain imaging revealed an improvement in the previously observed vasoconstriction of the M1–2 segments of both MCAs and the P1–2 segments of both PCAs, remaining as mild narrowing at the proximal M2 segment of the right MCA (Fig. 1C,D). Three months after the injection, MRA brain imaging revealed an improvement in the previously observed mild narrowing at the proximal M2 segment of the right MCA (Fig. 2A). Six months after the injection, MRA brain imaging revealed further improvement of the mild stenosis at the proximal M2 segment of the right MCA (Fig. 2B) but was still not back to normal. She did not receive any specific medications for RCVS.

Discussion

In Thailand, anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies are only available in hospitals and physicians’ clinics. At this centre, pharmacists dispense monoclonal antibodies to patients, and the nurses at the outpatient department do the injection. Patients are observed for 30 min after the injection because of a hypersensitivity reaction warning on the Food and Drug Administration label. According to the Thai guideline [4], physicians should advise patients to prospectively record their headaches in the logbook headache diary to monitor the effectiveness of anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies.This patient developed a transient focal sensory neurological deficit without headache 10 min after the loading dose of galcanezumab, and the symptoms spread gradually over at least 5 min and then spontaneously recovered within 2 days. A transient focal neurological deficit might be a presentation of migraine aura or reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome. According to the International Classification of Headache Disorders [5], the diagnostic criteria of migraine aura are the following: at least two attacks fulfilling criteria B and C; criteria B is one or more of the following fully reversible aura symptoms, including visual, sensory, speech and/or language, motor, brainstem, or retinal; criteria C is at least three of the following six characteristics, including at least one aura symptom spreading gradually over at least 5 min, two or more aura symptoms occurring in succession, each individual aura symptom lasting 5–60 min, at least one aura symptom being unilateral, at least one aura symptom being positive, or the aura being accompanied by or followed within 60 min by headache [5]. The diagnostic criteria of RCVS are the following: any new headache fulfilling criteria C; criteria B is RCVS have been diagnosed; criteria C is evidence of causation demonstrated by either or both of the following: headache with or without focal deficits and/or seizure has led to angiography with string-of-beads appearance and diagnosis of RCVS or the headache has one or more of the following characteristics, including thunderclap onset, triggered by sexual activity, exertion, Valsalva manoeuvres, emotion, bathing and/or showering, or present or recurrent for up to 1 month after onset with no new significant headache after > 1 month [5]. A large series of patients with confirmed RCVS have shown that up to 75% present with headache as the only symptom, but the condition can be associated with fluctuating focal neurological deficits and sometimes seizures. Headache is absent in a minority of cases of RCVS. RCVS is also diagnosed based on the key clinical features of thunderclap headache, cerebral vasoconstriction on imaging in at least two different arteries, and resolution of vasoconstriction by 3 months in the absence of primary angiitis of the central nervous system or aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage [6]. This patient had only one attack of fully reversible symptoms of sensory abnormality, and the symptoms spread gradually over at least 5 min and were unilateral but lasted > 60 min. The sensory symptoms were not classified as persistent aura without infarction because of a shorter duration of < 1 week. They may represent prolonged migraine aura without headache, as her symptoms last for > 1 h and < 7 days [7]. Her MRA brain imaging was not consistent with the typical string-of-beads appearance but demonstrated cerebral arterial vasoconstriction in more than two different arteries. However, there were no supporting examination data, such as transcranial Doppler, which might provide additional information on the progression and improvement of the vasoconstriction. For RCVS, the vasoconstriction demonstrated in MRA brain imaging resolves by 3 months, but the patient's MRA brain imaging showed residual vasoconstriction by the end of the 3rd month. This patient did not have a headache or any triggers typical for RCVS. Moreover, the focal neurological symptoms developed within 10 min after the loading dose of galcanezumab, which seemed too rapid to draw a connection. A pharmacokinetic study revealed that galcanezumab concentrations rose and reached peak levels 5 days post-dose [8]. Her symptoms developed too early after the injection. However, galcanezumab might produce clinical features that mimic RCVS, which needs further research. There have been reports of RCVS with non-thunderclap headaches or nonspecific headaches, called a variant of the RCVS [9, 10], or no headaches, [10, 11], which is an unusual case of RCVS [12, 13]. Typically, RCVS can present with normal neurological findings [12] and a benign and self-limited condition [12, 14], with resolution within days to weeks [15]. There have been no reports of bilateral non-pulsatile tinnitus in the literature. Non-pulsatile tinnitus is usually otologic in origin; i.e.: ear infections or hearing loss; whereas pulsatile tinnitus can be caused by issues with circulation. RCVS is a very rare side effect of anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies. Most patients develop RCVS secondary to various triggers, particularly medications, including triptans and ergot derivatives [15, 16]. This patient took the last dose of acetaminophen 500 mg plus eletriptan 40 mg 22 days before the injection. Thus, the MRA changes would not be the effect of eletriptan. The total clearance of eletriptan from the body is five half-lives of approximately 4 h. The MRA brain imaging in this patient still had residual constriction by the end of the 6th month. Her abnormal MRA brain imaging might have been due to the effects of an anti-CGRP monoclonal antibody. Anti-CGRP medications like galcanezumab have a half-life of about 30 days and a washout period of 5-6 months. As a result, it is possible to have an ongoing vasoconstrictive effect secondary to anti-CGRP medications 6 months post loading dose. For RCVS, one previous report provided information about only elderly patients treated with erenumab [2]. Another study reported a thunderclap headache 10 days after the second injection of 70 mg of erenumab and a computed tomography angiogram compatible with RCVS [3]. Another study reported a case of RCVS after 8 days of fremanezumab administration. Six days and 8 days after fremanezumab administration, the patient developed refractory headaches and left-sided neglect, left hemiparesis, and bilateral visual field impairment, respectively. Computed tomography angiography showed multiple areas of segmental stenosis in the anterior and posterior circulation [17]. An in vitro study of fremanezumab, an anti-CGRP monoclonal antibody, confirmed that it inhibits the vasodilatory effect induced by both human and rat CGRP [18]. The antibody may reach the perivascular sensory synaptic cleft and block the vasodilatory response of released CGRP in the present in vitro model [18]. Thus, CGRP neutralization might have the mechanistic potential to block vasoprotective responses and cause severe vasoconstriction [18]. A postulate mechanism for RCVS would be blocking the function of the CGRP on smooth muscle cells of cerebral arteries [19] to inhibit reflex vasodilatation [20]. A possible mechanism of this case would be related to a potential interference of CGRP blockade with BBB disruption [21]Limitations: It is possible that there are other undiagnosed sources of vasoconstriction in this case that may have been triggered by the addition of an anti-CGRP antibody, such as a dysautonomias.

Conclusions

This case raises a concern for cerebral artery vasoconstriction after anti-CGRP treatment. Providers need to be aware of this possibility, which may present as prolonged aura and may mimic RCVS.

Patient Perspective

The patient was concerned about the numbness and how serious the symptoms were. After the symptom resolution, she thought that it was a side effect of the medication.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 40-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Transient numbness of right upper and lower extremities and right face lasting 2 days, occurring after galcanezumab loading dose.
- **History of Present Illness:** Presented transient right-sided numbness without headache 10 minutes post-injection, which resolved over 2 days. Experienced bilateral non-pulsatile tinnitus starting the next day, resolving after 2 weeks.
- **Past Medical History:** High-frequency episodic migraines with visual aura for 6 years; comorbid allergic rhinitis. Previous trials of various migraine preventatives, including propranolol, topiramate, and nortriptyline, with limited efficacy.
- **Personal History:** None pertinent to this condition.
- **Family History:** Not mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Normal neurological findings, including pinprick sensation, motor function, and deep tendon reflexes.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) revealed segmental arterial constriction in bilateral middle cerebral arteries (M1–M2) and posterior cerebral arteries (P1–P2). Partial resolution was observed at 1, 3, and 6 months post-injection.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Prolonged migraine sensory aura without headache:**
   - Considered due to reversible sensory deficit lasting more than 1 hour but less than 7 days.
   - Excluded because typical features of aura (e.g., headache following aura within 60 minutes) were absent.

2. **Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS):**
   - Considered based on segmental arterial vasoconstriction in multiple arteries on MRA, a hallmark sign of RCVS.
   - Partially excluded because the patient's symptoms were atypical for RCVS (e.g., absence of thunderclap headache and a longer than expected time for vasoconstriction resolution).

3. **Cerebral vasoconstriction secondary to galcanezumab:**
   - Highly probable due to the temporal association with galcanezumab administration within 10 minutes and prolonged vasoconstrictive effects.
   - Supported by partial resolution of vasoconstriction over 6 months and known potential vasoconstrictive effects of anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Cerebral vasoconstriction secondary to galcanezumab administration. This was concluded based on the rapid onset of symptoms post-injection, MRA findings of cerebral arterial vasoconstriction, and the exclusion of other plausible conditions such as prolonged migraine aura or typical RCVS.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Cerebral vasoconstriction secondary to galcanezumab administration.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient presented with transient right-sided numbness affecting the face and extremities shortly after receiving a galcanezumab loading dose. This temporal relationship is highly suggestive of a potential drug-related adverse event. The symptoms were transient, resolving within two days, which is important to note.  The subsequent development of bilateral tinnitus, also resolving, further suggests a neurological impact, possibly related to vascular changes.

<step 2> **Review Past Medical History:**  The patient has a history of episodic migraines with aura, for which galcanezumab was prescribed.  Her migraine history is relevant as it places her in a population treated with this medication.  The limited efficacy of previous migraine preventatives might suggest more severe or refractory migraines. Allergic rhinitis is also present, but its direct relevance to the neurological symptoms is less clear, although it could suggest a predisposition to hypersensitivity reactions.

<step 3> **Evaluate Physical Examination Findings:** A normal neurological examination after the resolution of symptoms is reassuring but does not exclude transient neurological events.  It suggests that there are no persistent neurological deficits.

<step 4> **Interpret Ancillary Test Results (MRA):** The MRA findings are the most critical piece of information. The discovery of segmental arterial constriction in bilateral MCAs and PCAs strongly indicates cerebral vasospasm. The partial resolution observed over months confirms that the vasoconstriction is reversible, which is a key feature of certain vascular conditions.

<step 5> **Integrate all findings and consider potential diagnoses:** The combination of transient neurological symptoms (numbness, tinnitus), temporal association with galcanezumab injection, and MRA evidence of reversible cerebral vasoconstriction points towards **Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS)**.  While RCVS is often associated with thunderclap headache, it can present with other neurological symptoms, including transient focal neurological deficits like numbness, and tinnitus can also be a feature. Drug-induced RCVS is a recognized entity, and medications, even those like galcanezumab which are not typically associated with vasoconstriction, can trigger it in susceptible individuals.

<step 6> **Formulate the Final Diagnosis:** Based on the clinical picture and particularly the MRA findings, the most likely diagnosis is Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS). The temporal association with galcanezumab strongly suggests that the medication was a trigger in this case, making it likely **Drug-Induced Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome** specifically associated with galcanezumab.

### Answer:
Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS), likely drug-induced by galcanezumab.

This temporal relationship is highly suggestive of a potential drug-related adverse event. The symptoms were transient, resolving within two days, which is important to note.  The subsequent development of bilateral tinnitus, also resolving, further suggests a neurological impact, possibly related to vascular changes.

**Review Past Medical History:**  The patient has a history of episodic migraines with aura, for which galcanezumab was prescribed.  Her migraine history is relevant as it places her in a population treated with this medication.  The limited efficacy of previous migraine preventatives might suggest more severe or refractory migraines. Allergic rhinitis is also present, but its direct relevance to the neurological symptoms is less clear, although it could suggest a predisposition to hypersensitivity reactions.

**Evaluate Physical Examination Findings:** A normal neurological examination after the resolution of symptoms is reassuring but does not exclude transient neurological events.  It suggests that there are no persistent neurological deficits.

**Interpret Ancillary Test Results (MRA):** The MRA findings are the most critical piece of information. The discovery of segmental arterial constriction in bilateral MCAs and PCAs strongly indicates cerebral vasospasm. The partial resolution observed over months confirms that the vasoconstriction is reversible, which is a key feature of certain vascular conditions.

**Integrate all findings and consider potential diagnoses:** The combination of transient neurological symptoms (numbness, tinnitus), temporal association with galcanezumab injection, and MRA evidence of reversible cerebral vasoconstriction points towards **Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS)**.  While RCVS is often associated with thunderclap headache, it can present with other neurological symptoms, including transient focal neurological deficits like numbness, and tinnitus can also be a feature. Drug-induced RCVS is a recognized entity, and medications, even those like galcanezumab which are not typically associated with vasoconstriction, can trigger it in susceptible individuals.

**Formulate the Final Diagnosis:** Based on the clinical picture and particularly the MRA findings, the most likely diagnosis is Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS). The temporal association with galcanezumab strongly suggests that the medication was a trigger in this case, making it likely **Drug-Induced Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome** specifically associated with galcanezumab.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Prolonged migraine sensory aura without headache considered due to reversible sensory deficit lasting more than 1 hour but less than 7 days; excluded due to absence of typical aura features (e.g., headache following aura within 60 minutes).>

<Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) considered based on segmental arterial vasoconstriction in multiple arteries on MRA; partially excluded due to atypical symptoms (e.g., absence of thunderclap headache and longer than expected vasoconstriction resolution).>

<Cerebral vasoconstriction secondary to galcanezumab highly probable due to temporal association with galcanezumab administration within 10 minutes and prolonged vasoconstrictive effects; supported by partial resolution of vasoconstriction over 6 months and known potential vasoconstrictive effects of anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies.>

<Final diagnosis: Cerebral vasoconstriction secondary to galcanezumab administration concluded based on rapid onset of symptoms post-injection, MRA findings of cerebral arterial vasoconstriction, and exclusion of other plausible conditions such as prolonged migraine aura or typical RCVS.>
